exam_overview.exam_name: AP United States Government and Politics

sections.name: Multiple Choice
sections.notes: Majority of questions are stimulus-based (text passages, data, SCOTUS excerpts, political cartoons, charts)

sections.name: Free Response
frq_types: Concept Application (1 question) — apply a concept to a real-world scenario
Quantitative Analysis (1 question) — interpret a data set, chart, or map
SCOTUS Comparison (1 question) — compare a non-required case to a required case
Argument Essay (1 question) — construct a defensible claim using evidence from required documents/cases

required_documents: Constitution of the United States (including all Amendments)
Letter from Birmingham Jail (Martin Luther King Jr.)
required_scotus_cases: Marbury v. Madison (1803)
McCulloch v. Maryland (1819)
Schenck v. United States (1919)
Brown v. Board of Education (1954)
Baker v. Carr (1962)
Engel v. Vitale (1962)
Gideon v. Wainwright (1963)
Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District (1969)
New York Times Co. v. United States (1971)
Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972)
Roe v. Wade (1973)
Shaw v. Reno (1993)
United States v. Lopez (1995)
McDonald v. City of Chicago (2010)
Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010)
tips: Know each required SCOTUS case by: parties, holding, constitutional provision at issue, and significance — the SCOTUS Comparison FRQ will always use a required case as the anchor
Know each required Foundational Document by: author/origin, core argument, and which constitutional provisions it influenced or criticized
The Argument Essay requires citing at least one required document or case as evidence — practice pairing documents with constitutional principles
Stimulus-based MCQs require you to read the source AND apply outside knowledge — do not rely on the passage alone
Quantitative Analysis FRQ: describe the data trend first, then explain using course concepts — do not skip the explanation step

units.name: Foundations of American Democracy
key_topics: Social contract theory and Enlightenment influences on the Founding (Locke, Montesquieu)
Weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation and causes of the Constitutional Convention
Constitutional Convention compromises: Great Compromise, Three-Fifths Compromise
Federalism: enumerated, implied, reserved, and concurrent powers
Necessary and Proper Clause and Supremacy Clause
Federalist vs. Anti-Federalist debate over ratification
Federalist No. 10 (factions and large republic), Federalist No. 51 (separation of powers)
Brutus No. 1 (Anti-Federalist critique of federal power)
Types of democracy: participatory, pluralist, elite
Fiscal federalism: categorical grants, block grants, unfunded mandates
learning_objectives: LOR-1.A: Explain how democratic ideals and the constitutional framework set out by the Founders have evolved over time
CON-1.A: Explain how the Framers constructed the Constitution to address competing interests
CON-1.B: Explain the ongoing debate over the appropriate balance of power between national and state governments
CON-2.A: Explain how the Constitution structures government and limits its power
CON-2.B: Explain how the separation of powers and checks and balances prevent tyranny
required_scotus_cases: McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) — upheld implied powers and Supremacy Clause; Necessary and Proper Clause allows federal action beyond enumerated powers
United States v. Lopez (1995) — limited Commerce Clause; Congress cannot regulate non-economic activity without a substantial effect on interstate commerce
common_question_types: Stimulus-based MCQ comparing Federalist No. 10 or Brutus No. 1 to a modern scenario
FRQ Concept Application: apply federalism (categorical vs. block grants, unfunded mandates) to a policy scenario
Argument Essay using Federalist No. 10 or Brutus No. 1 as evidence for a claim about constitutional design
Comparative MCQ distinguishing participatory, pluralist, and elite theories of democracy
exam_tips: Federalist No. 10 = Madison's argument that a large republic with many factions prevents majority tyranny; Federalist No. 51 = Madison's argument that ambition checks ambition through separation of powers — memorize which number corresponds to which argument
Brutus No. 1 is always paired with the Anti-Federalist concern that the Necessary and Proper Clause grants unlimited federal power — the correct counter-argument is Federalist No. 10/51
McCulloch v. Maryland tests the Necessary and Proper Clause AND the Supremacy Clause together — know both holdings

units.name: Interactions Among Branches of Government
key_topics: Congressional structure: bicameralism, House vs. Senate rules, leadership roles
Legislative process: committee system, filibuster and cloture, conference committees
Congressional powers: taxing, spending, war powers, oversight, confirmation
Models of representation: delegate, trustee, politico
Formal and informal presidential powers (veto, executive orders, signing statements, bully pulpit)
Bureaucracy: rulemaking, delegated authority, iron triangles, congressional oversight
Judicial review and the role of the federal courts
Federalist No. 70 (unitary executive) and Federalist No. 78 (judicial independence)
Redistricting, gerrymandering, and reapportionment
Interest groups and policy influence (iron triangles, issue networks)
learning_objectives: CON-3.A: Explain how Congress is organized and how it performs its functions
CON-4.A: Explain how the President's formal and informal powers have changed over time
CON-5.A: Explain how the federal courts impact policy
CON-6.A: Explain how the bureaucracy carries out the responsibilities of the federal government
PRD-1.A: Explain how interest groups attempt to influence policymaking
required_scotus_cases: Marbury v. Madison (1803) — established judicial review; Supreme Court has authority to strike down laws that violate the Constitution
Baker v. Carr (1962) — ruled that legislative apportionment is a justiciable political question; opened federal courts to redistricting challenges
Shaw v. Reno (1993) — racial gerrymandering solely for majority-minority districts may violate the Equal Protection Clause
common_question_types: Stimulus-based MCQ analyzing a presidential action and identifying whether it is a formal or informal power
SCOTUS Comparison FRQ anchored on Marbury v. Madison or Baker v. Carr
Quantitative Analysis FRQ using congressional data (e.g., incumbency rates, party voting unity)
Concept Application FRQ on iron triangles or congressional-bureaucratic relationships
exam_tips: Marbury v. Madison is the most tested SCOTUS case on the exam — always connect it to judicial review, separation of powers, and Federalist No. 78

Distinguish formal presidential powers (enumerated in Article II: veto, commander-in-chief, treaty, appointments) from informal powers (executive orders, signing statements, bully pulpit) — the exam tests this distinction repeatedly

Federalist No. 70 argues for a single, energetic executive; Federalist No. 78 argues that the judiciary's lifetime tenure makes it the 'least dangerous branch' — both are frequently quoted in stimulus passages

units.name: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights
key_topics: Civil liberties vs. civil rights distinction
Selective incorporation of the Bill of Rights via the 14th Amendment Due Process Clause
First Amendment freedoms: speech, religion (Establishment and Free Exercise Clauses), press, assembly
Second Amendment and incorporation (McDonald v. City of Chicago)
Fourth Amendment, exclusionary rule, and unreasonable search and seizure
Fifth and Sixth Amendment due process protections
Equal Protection Clause and three tiers of scrutiny (strict, intermediate, rational basis)
Civil rights movement, de jure vs. de facto segregation, Civil Rights Act of 1964
Letter from Birmingham Jail as a primary source on civil disobedience
learning_objectives: LOR-2.A: Explain how the Bill of Rights limits government power
LOR-2.B: Explain how the 14th Amendment extended constitutional protections to state action through incorporation
LOR-3.A: Explain how the Equal Protection Clause has driven the expansion of civil rights
LOR-3.B: Explain how movements, legislation, and court decisions have expanded civil rights for historically marginalized groups
required_documents: Letter from Birmingham Jail (Martin Luther King Jr.)
Constitution of the United States — 1st, 4th, 5th, 6th, 14th Amendments
required_scotus_cases: Schenck v. United States (1919) — established 'clear and present danger' test for government limits on free speech
Brown v. Board of Education (1954) — overturned Plessy v. Ferguson; racially segregated schools are inherently unequal under Equal Protection Clause
Engel v. Vitale (1962) — school-sponsored prayer violates the Establishment Clause
Gideon v. Wainwright (1963) — Sixth Amendment right to counsel is incorporated; states must provide attorneys to indigent defendants
Tinker v. Des Moines Independent Community School District (1969) — students retain First Amendment rights; symbolic speech is protected unless substantially disruptive
New York Times Co. v. United States (1971) — prior restraint is presumptively unconstitutional; government must meet heavy burden to block publication
Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972) — Free Exercise Clause protects Amish families from compulsory education law
McDonald v. City of Chicago (2010) — Second Amendment is incorporated against the states
common_question_types: Stimulus-based MCQ using a SCOTUS excerpt to apply civil liberties doctrine
SCOTUS Comparison FRQ anchored on Tinker v. Des Moines, Engel v. Vitale, or Gideon v. Wainwright
Concept Application FRQ distinguishing civil liberties from civil rights, or applying the incorporation doctrine
Argument Essay using Letter from Birmingham Jail or Brown v. Board of Education as evidence
exam_tips: Always distinguish civil liberties (protection FROM government action) from civil rights (equal treatment BY government) — the exam frequently mixes them in stimulus passages
Know the three-tier scrutiny framework: strict scrutiny for race/national origin (compelling interest + narrow tailoring); intermediate scrutiny for gender; rational basis for economic regulations

For the SCOTUS Comparison FRQ, you must identify both the similarity and the difference between the required case and the presented case — practice applying Tinker's 'substantial disruption' test to new scenarios

units.name: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs
key_topics: American political culture: liberty, equality, free enterprise, rule of law
Political socialization agents: family, school, peers, religion, media, major events
Generational and lifecycle effects on political attitudes
Liberal, conservative, and libertarian ideological positions
Party identification as the strongest predictor of vote choice
Public opinion polling: random sampling, margin of error, question wording, question order effects
Valence issues vs. wedge issues
Fiscal policy (taxation/spending) vs. monetary policy (Federal Reserve)
learning_objectives: PRD-2.A: Explain how political culture and socialization influence political attitudes
PRD-2.B: Explain how various agents of political socialization contribute to ideology formation
PRD-3.A: Explain the difference between liberal and conservative ideological positions
MPA-1.A: Explain how polling measures and shapes public opinion
MPA-2.A: Explain how public opinion data can be used to understand political behavior
common_question_types: Quantitative Analysis FRQ using a polling data table or ideological distribution chart
Stimulus-based MCQ interpreting a survey graph and identifying sampling or question-wording issues
Concept Application FRQ connecting political socialization agents to specific ideological outcomes
Comparative MCQ distinguishing liberal, conservative, and libertarian positions on a policy issue
exam_tips: Polling methodology is a tested skill — know the difference between sampling error (statistical, unavoidable), non-response bias (systematic), and question-wording effects (design flaw)
Party identification is formed early through family socialization and is more durable than issue positions — this is the key contrast with issue voting
Unit 4 has no required documents or SCOTUS cases, but it frequently pairs with Units 1 and 5 in FRQ scenarios about democratic theory and voter behavior

units.name: Political Participation
key_topics: Constitutional amendments expanding voting rights: 15th (1870), 19th (1920), 24th (1964), 26th (1971)
Voting Rights Act of 1965 and barriers to political participation
Electoral College structure, winner-take-all rules, and swing state dynamics
Two-party system and Duverger's Law (plurality voting produces two-party systems)
Campaign finance: FECA, BCRA (McCain-Feingold), Citizens United v. FEC (2010)
Political parties: functions, party identification, party platform, realignment
Interest groups: lobbying strategies, PACs, Super PACs, 501(c)(4) organizations
Media roles: agenda-setting, framing, priming, new media fragmentation
Incumbency advantage in congressional elections
learning_objectives: PRD-5.A: Explain how constitutional provisions and federal law have expanded voting rights over time
MPA-3.A: Explain how political parties influence elections and governance
MPA-4.A: Explain how interest groups attempt to influence elections and policy
MPA-5.A: Explain the role of media in influencing political attitudes and behavior
required_documents: Constitution of the United States — 15th, 19th, 24th, 26th Amendments
required_scotus_cases: Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission (2010) — corporations and unions have First Amendment rights to make unlimited independent political expenditures; created Super PACs
common_question_types: Quantitative Analysis FRQ using voter turnout data, party identification trends, or Electoral College maps
Stimulus-based MCQ using a campaign finance scenario to identify Super PAC vs. direct contribution rules
Concept Application FRQ on interest group strategies or media agenda-setting
SCOTUS Comparison FRQ anchored on Citizens United v. FEC
exam_tips: Citizens United is always connected to the First Amendment — the holding is that corporations and unions have free speech rights for independent expenditures, not direct contributions; direct contributions are still regulated
Know the four constitutional amendments that expanded voting rights in chronological order and what barrier each removed: 15th (race), 19th (sex), 24th (poll taxes), 26th (age 18)

The Argument Essay in FRQ Section 2 frequently uses Unit 5 content — practice constructing arguments about electoral system design (Electoral College, gerrymandering, campaign finance) using required documents as evidence
